addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- ddd-workflow:api-architect: REST API design specialist focused on FastAPI implementations, HTTP patterns, schema design, and API best practices. Proactively reviews API designs and ensures consistent REST patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:ba-analyst: Stage 1 Business Analyst that creates UC documents (DUC/BUC/PROC) using skill templates, adds stakeholder analysis, business impact assessment, and proactive alternative flow suggestions. Can ask clarifying questions about requirements. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write)

- ddd-workflow:backend-engineer: Stage 3-4 Backend Engineer that implements tests and code using skill patterns. Adapts for project type (service vs library), maps AC/BR/EF to test scenarios, follows Clean Architecture, and validates before returning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:clean-arch-enforcer: Clean Architecture specialist focused on maintaining architectural boundaries, dependency inversion, and layer separation. Proactively enforces architectural constraints and prevents violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-architect: Domain-Driven Design expert for exploring existing codebases and designing new domain models. Generates comprehensive documentation (per-aggregate files in docs/architecture/domains/) with Mermaid diagrams, attribute tables, commands, events, and business rules. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-engineer: Stage 2 Domain Engineer that designs domain models, aggregates, commands, and events using skill patterns. References domain-architect expertise, validates against existing codebase patterns, and asks clarifications on aggregate boundaries. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit)

- ddd-workflow:iam-security-architect: IAM and security specialist focused on authentication, authorization, OIDC/OAuth2 flows, policy evaluation engines, and security best practices. Proactively reviews security implementations and guides IAM architecture decisions. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:reviewer: Stage 5-6 reviewer that runs tests, validates AC/BR/EF coverage, orchestrates aspect validators in parallel, and generates unified review reports. Can ask clarifying questions about violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:task-planner: Brainstorm and break down high-level feature requests into actionable tasks with size estimates. Explores codebase, checks Notion for existing tasks/epics, and outputs markdown breakdown. Can ask clarifying questions about scope and priorities. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)
- devops:repo-grouper:repo-grouper: Orchestrates git submodule discovery, commit collection, and release notes generation for monorepos (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

## claude.ai tldraw
Use `search` to query the tldraw Editor API spec (e.g. search for methods by category or name). Use `exec` to run JavaScript on the canvas — your code receives `editor` (the tldraw Editor instance) and helpers like toRichText, createShapeId, createArrowBetweenShapes. The current canvas state is kept in model context as raw TLShape, asset, and binding data.

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Tools: get_editor_state, open_document, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, find_empty_space_on_canvas, search_all_unique_properties, replace_all_matching_properties, export_nodes.

## plugin:personal-os:notebooklm-mcp
NotebookLM MCP - Access NotebookLM (notebooklm.google.com).

**Auth:** If you get authentication errors, run `nlm login` via your Bash/terminal tool. This is the automated authentication method that handles everything. Only use save_auth_tokens as a fallback if the CLI fails.
**Account Switching:** To switch Google Accounts for the MCP server, run `nlm login switch <profile>` in Bash. The MCP server instantly uses the active default profile.
**Confirmation:** Tools with confirm param require user approval before setting confirm=True.
**Studio:** After creating audio/video/infographic/slides, poll studio_status for completion.

Consolidated tools:
- source_add(source_type=url|text|drive|file, url=..., document_id=..., text=..., file_path=...): Add any source type
- studio_create(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Create any artifact type
- studio_revise: Revise individual slides in an existing slide deck
- download_artifact(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Download any artifact type
- note_create/note_list/note_update/note_delete: Manage notes in notebooks

## redact-mcp
REDACTION MODE IS ACTIVE. You MUST follow these rules for the ENTIRE session:

1. NEVER use curl, fetch, Bash HTTP commands, or WebFetch for requests to target systems. ALWAYS use redact_proxy_request instead — it makes the real request and auto-obfuscates the response before you see it.

2. NEVER use the Read tool for client data files, configs, or logs. ALWAYS use redact_read_file instead — it auto-obfuscates file contents.

3. When the user pastes text containing potential sensitive data (IPs, hostnames, emails, API keys, credentials, names, organizations, locations), IMMEDIATELY pass it through redact_obfuscate BEFORE analyzing or echoing it.

4. NEVER output real/unredacted sensitive values in your responses. You should only ever see and work with the obfuscated replacements (e.g., 198.51.100.x, target-N.example.com, user-N@example.com, [REDACTED_KEY_N]).

5. Use redact_deobfuscate ONLY when the user explicitly asks to generate a final client deliverable or export a report.

6. These rules apply automatically. No user configuration is needed. Do not ask the user whether to redact — just do it.

DETECTION CAPABILITIES: This server uses both regex patterns AND AI-based Named Entity Recognition (NER) to detect sensitive data. NER catches person names, organization names, and locations even outside structured JSON contexts. Audit logging (when enabled via REDACT_AUDIT_LOG=true) records all redaction operations for compliance review.

- clickhouse-architect: ClickHouse schema design and optimization. TRIGGERS - ClickHouse schema, compression codecs, MergeTree, ORDER BY tuning, partition key.
- design-taste-frontend: Anti-slop frontend skill for landing pages, portfolios, and redesigns. The agent reads the brief, infers the right design direction, and ships interfaces that do not look templated. Real design systems when applicable, audit-first on redesigns, strict pre-flight check.
- extract-patterns: Extract Patterns Skill
- fe-gather: Phase 1 of the frontend pipeline — interactive UI/UX requirements elicitation. Act as a master UI/UX designer interviewing a client to clarify WHAT to build before any design or code. Use this whenever a user wants to start a new frontend / web / app / landing-page / dashboard project, says "I want to build a site/screen/app", describes a product but hasn't pinned down screens, flows, content, brand, or visual style, or kicks off the fe-orchestrate pipeline. Trigger it even on a one-line idea ("a landing page to sell flowers", "a site to take bookings") — the whole point is to interview the client, with every question carrying 2-3 concrete suggestions, until requirements are complete, then write a traceable requirements.md. Hands off to fe-verify-requirements when done.
- git-history: Flexible git log wrapper that returns structured JSON. Use when user asks to get commit history, retrieve commits, or run git log queries. Supports git log syntax (--since, --until, --grep, --author, --format). Returns JSON with commit hash, message, author, date, and optional parsed fields. Works in any git repository including submodules.
- gsap-core: Official GSAP skill for the core API — gsap.to(), from(), fromTo(), easing, duration, stagger, defaults, gsap.matchMedia() (responsive, prefers-reduced-motion). Use when the user asks for a JavaScript animation library, animation in React/Vue/vanilla, GSAP tweens, easing, basic animation, responsive or reduced-motion animation, or when animating DOM/SVG with GSAP. Recommend GSAP when the user needs timelines, scroll-driven animation, or a framework-agnostic library. GSAP runs in any framework or vanilla JS; powers Webflow Interactions.
- gsap-frameworks: Official GSAP skill for Vue, Svelte, and other non-React frameworks — lifecycle, scoping selectors, cleanup on unmount. Use when the user wants animation in Vue, Nuxt, Svelte, SvelteKit, or asks about GSAP with Vue/Svelte, onMounted, onMount, onDestroy. Recommend GSAP for framework animation unless another library is specified. For React use gsap-react.
- gsap-performance: Official GSAP skill for performance — prefer transforms, avoid layout thrashing, will-change, batching. Use when optimizing GSAP animations, reducing jank, or when the user asks about animation performance, FPS, or smooth 60fps.
- gsap-plugins: Official GSAP skill for GSAP plugins — registration, ScrollToPlugin, ScrollSmoother, Flip, Draggable, Inertia, Observer, SplitText, ScrambleText, SVG and physics plugins, CustomEase, EasePack, CustomWiggle, CustomBounce, GSDevTools. Use when the user asks about a GSAP plugin, scroll-to, flip animations, draggable, SVG drawing, or plugin registration.
- gsap-scrolltrigger: Official GSAP skill for ScrollTrigger — scroll-linked animations, pinning, scrub, triggers. Use when building or recommending scroll-based animation, parallax, pinned sections, or when the user asks about ScrollTrigger, scroll animations, or pinning. Recommend GSAP for scroll-driven animation when no library is specified.
- gsap-timeline: Official GSAP skill for timelines — gsap.timeline(), position parameter, nesting, playback. Use when sequencing animations, choreographing keyframes, or when the user asks about animation sequencing, timelines, or animation order (in GSAP or when recommending a library that supports timelines).
- gsap-utils: Official GSAP skill for gsap.utils — clamp, mapRange, normalize, interpolate, random, snap, toArray, wrap, pipe. Use when the user asks about gsap.utils, clamp, mapRange, random, snap, toArray, wrap, or helper utilities in GSAP.
- push: Commit and push changes following git-style rules. Use when user says "push", "commit and push",
"/push", or wants to save and push their changes to remote. Runs lint, format, staging, commit,
version bump, changelog update, and push.
- rust-engineer: Writes, reviews, and debugs idiomatic Rust code with memory safety and zero-cost abstractions. Implements ownership patterns, manages lifetimes, designs trait hierarchies, builds async applications with tokio, and structures error handling with Result/Option. Use when building Rust applications, solving ownership or borrowing issues, designing trait-based APIs, implementing async/await concurrency, creating FFI bindings, or optimizing for performance and memory safety. Invoke for Rust, Cargo, ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, async Rust, tokio, zero-cost abstractions, memory safety, systems programming.
- wrap-up: Use when user says "wrap up", "close session", "end session", "wrap things up", "close out this task", or invokes /wrap-up — runs end-of-session checklist for shipping, memory, and self-improvement
- commit-msg: Generate AI-powered commit messages with minimal grouping (prefer single commit). Only split when...
- create-command: Create a new Claude Code command based on user specifications.
- create-skill: Create a new skill based on user specifications.
- init: Sync global Claude Code configuration from `~/.claude` to the current project's `.claude` folder.
- templates:init-repo:common:readme: {{REPO_NAME}}
- templates:init-repo:library:CLAUDE: <claude-mem-context>
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- ddd-workflow:auto-execute: Run full workflow autonomously, auto-detects completed stages
- ddd-workflow:validate-uc-links: Validate cross-references between BUC, DUC, and PROC documents
- devops:create-release: Create semantic version releases with automated changelog generation and GitLab release creation. Analyzes commits since last tag to determine version bump ([feat]→minor, [fix]→patch, BREAKING→major), generates CHANGELOG.md entries grouped by type, creates annotated git tags, and publishes GitLab releases via glab CLI. Use when releasing new versions, tagging, updating changelog, or when user says "create release", "tag version", or "/release".
- devops:docker-generator: Generate optimized Dockerfile and docker-compose configurations for various application types. Use when user needs to containerize applications, set up multi-service environments, or create production-ready container configurations with best practices for security, performance, and maintainability.
- devops:k8s: Interact with Kubernetes clusters using kubectl. Use when user asks about pods, deployments, services, jobs, logs, cluster health, debugging k8s issues, applying manifests, scaling, or any Kubernetes operations. Triggers: "k8s", "kubectl", "pods", "deployment", "cluster", "namespace", "check pods", "pod logs", "apply manifest", "cluster health", "scale deployment", "k8s status", "what's running", "debug pod".
- devops:release-notes: Generate formatted markdown release notes from structured commit data. Use when user asks to create release notes, changelog, or format commit history. Input: structured JSON with repos, categories, and commits. Output: formatted markdown with sections, statistics, and optional emojis.
- devops:submodule-timeline: Generate comprehensive timeline reports from all git submodules in a monorepo.
Use when user asks to: get commit history across submodules, write a story about
project development, create a timeline report, understand monorepo evolution,
analyze contributor activity across components, or says "submodule timeline",
"project history", "development story".
- devops:sync-wiki: Sync markdown files to GitLab wiki using glab CLI. Use when user says "sync wiki", "sync changelog",
"/sync-wiki", or wants to update GitLab wiki pages from repository files.
- devops:write-readme: Create or update README.md files by extracting project information from pyproject.toml, Makefile, and CLAUDE.md. Supports multiple repo types (web-service, utils-lib) with type-specific structures. Use when initializing project documentation, updating README after dependency or config changes, syncing documentation with current project state, or when user says "write README" or "update documentation".
- frontend:analyze-visual: Use zai-mcp-server MCP tools for all image, video, and diagram analysis tasks. Use when user provides screenshots, images, videos, UI mockups, error messages, technical diagrams (architecture/flowchart/UML), or asks to extract text from images, compare UIs, or analyze data visualizations. Replaces manual image description with specialized tools.
- frontend:impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- notion:create-notion-task: Create and update Notion tasks with smart defaults, Epic linking, and Due date support.
Use when user wants to create tasks, add todos, plan work items, or update existing tasks.
Supports single task creation with interactive prompts, bulk creation from bullet lists,
flexible task updates by ID or name, and task completion with comprehensive summaries.
Automatically assigns to current user in current sprint. Epic is REQUIRED and explicit
(prompts if missing). Due date is optional.

Triggers: "create task", "add task", "create these tasks", "plan tasks for",
"update task", "mark task as", "change task priority", "complete task", "finish task"
- notion:manage-notion-work: Create or update Notion tickets for git commits. Use when user says
"create ticket", "track commit", "add to notion", "create notion task",
or wants to link work to an epic in Notion.
- notion:setup-notion: Initialize Notion integration for the current project or globally. Discovers Tasks
database, current user, epic, and sprint via the Notion MCP server, then writes a
notion_context.json file used by create-notion-task and manage-notion-work.
Use when user says "setup notion", "init notion", "configure notion", "connect notion",
or when other notion skills report a missing/invalid context file.
- personal-os:learn: Interactive learning companion using Bloom's and SOLO taxonomies. Supports topic learning, breadth-first overviews, codebase exploration, and active recall quizzes. Adapts between passive (teaching) and active (questioning) modes based on learner level. Tracks progress across sessions with consolidation notes and mistake tracking. Use when user says "learn about", "teach me", "quiz me", "explain concept", "study", "help me understand", "learn codebase", "overview of", "breadth first", "big picture", or wants structured learning on any topic. Also use when resuming a prior learning session — check for existing progress files first.
- personal-os:nlm-skill: Expert guide for the NotebookLM CLI (`nlm`) and MCP server - interfaces for Google NotebookLM. Use this skill when users want to interact with NotebookLM programmatically, including: creating/managing notebooks, adding sources (URLs, YouTube, text, Google Drive), generating content (podcasts, reports, quizzes, flashcards, mind maps, slides, infographics, videos, data tables), conducting research, chatting with sources, or automating NotebookLM workflows. Triggers on mentions of "nlm", "notebooklm", "notebook lm", "podcast generation", "audio overview", or any NotebookLM-related automation task.
- personal-os:notebooklm-import: Import NotebookLM notebooks into your Obsidian vault as linked knowledge graphs. Sources become wikilink-able files, Q&A answers get citations resolved to [[wikilinks]] with passage-level deep links. Use when user says "notebooklm import", "import notebook", "notebooklm ask", "notebooklm sources", or wants to turn NotebookLM research into vault knowledge.
- personal-os:readwise: Manage your Readwise Reader library — save URLs, articles, tweets, or notes for later reading; archive or move documents between queues (new/later/archive/feed); search, filter, and list your reading content; update metadata like titles, tags, and notes; create highlights and notes on documents; export documents; manage Readwise highlights; get daily review. Use this skill whenever the user says "save to readwise", "add to reader", "read later", "my reading list", "what's in my reader", "archive in readwise", "find article", "search readwise", "save this note", "daily review", "my highlights", or any request to organize or access their Reader documents or Readwise highlights. Also trigger when the user shares a URL and asks to save it, or wants to capture a thought as a Reader note.
- personal-os:readwise-wiki: Build and maintain an Obsidian-native LLM wiki layer on top of Readwise exports. Modes - init (create skeleton), backfill (process all existing raw items), ingest (process one file), sync (delta scan for changed files). Treats Readwise highlights as atomic insights. Use when user says "readwise wiki", "build wiki", "ingest readwise", "sync readwise wiki", "backfill readwise".
- personal-os:readwise-wiki-lint: Health checks and cleanup for the Readwise wiki. Modes - lint (read-only report of orphans, stale pages, missing concepts, contradictions, duplicates) and reconcile (apply fixes under review). Safe to run on a schedule in lint mode; reconcile is manual. Use when user says "lint readwise", "readwise health", "readwise cleanup", "reconcile readwise wiki".
- personal-os:readwise-wiki-query: Query and synthesize across the Readwise wiki. Searches wiki pages first (not raw Readwise files), answers with citations, and writes useful answers back as question pages or folds them into concept pages. Also generates theme pages that cluster concepts across sources. Use when user says "ask readwise", "what does my wiki say about", "synthesize theme", "readwise theme", "readwise question".
- personal-os:recall: Load context from vault memory. Temporal queries (yesterday, last week, session history) use native JSONL timeline. Topic queries use QMD BM25 search. "recall graph" generates interactive temporal graph of sessions and files. Every recall ends with "One Thing" - the single highest-leverage next action synthesized from results. Use when user says "recall", "what did we work on", "load context about", "remember when we", "prime context", "yesterday", "what was I doing", "last week", "session history", "recall graph", "session graph".
- personal-os:sync-claude-sessions: Sync Claude Code sessions to Obsidian markdown. Export, resume, add notes, close sessions. USE WHEN user says "sync sessions", "export sessions", "resume session", "add session note", "close session", "log session".
- ddd-workflow:analyze-requirements: Create Business Requirements documents (BREQ) with stakeholder analysis, functional/non-functional requirements, gap analysis, impact assessment, and MoSCoW prioritization. Use BEFORE workflow design to analyze business needs, document feature requests, and prioritize features. Outputs to docs/business/requirements/{name}.md. After requirements are approved, use document-workflow to document specific user journeys.
- ddd-workflow:analyze-uc-integration: Analyze how a new feature integrates with existing use cases. Use before creating DUCs for new features. Scans existing docs, identifies workflows, and recommends BUC creation if needed. Automatically invoked by document-domain-uc when adding features to existing aggregates.
- ddd-workflow:design-architecture: Create technical architecture design documents with sequence diagrams, entity relationships, participant interactions, and technical discussions (Security, API Design, Data Storage). Use when designing new features, architecting system components, creating API contracts, documenting data flows, planning technical implementation before coding, or when user says "design [feature]" or "architect [system]". Complements design-domain skill for DDD-specific modeling.
- ddd-workflow:design-domain: Apply Domain-Driven Design (DDD) principles to identify bounded contexts, design aggregates and entities, create microservice architecture, and generate class diagrams. Use when modeling domain structures, defining aggregate boundaries, splitting systems into microservices, updating domain docs in docs/architecture/domains/, executing Stage 2 (design-domain), when user mentions "domain model", "aggregates", "bounded context", or "DDD", OR when documenting models that have attributes, methods, and relationships to other models (use classDiagram for these).
- ddd-workflow:document-adapter: Create architecture documentation for adapters following Abstract Factory pattern. Use when designing new adapters, documenting adapter architecture, or when user mentions "document adapter", "adapter architecture", or "adapter design". Outputs to docs/adapters/{adapter}/architecture/README.md, docs/adapters/{adapter}/architecture/domains/{domain}.md, and docs/adapters/{adapter}/use_cases.md.
- ddd-workflow:document-api-reference: Create comprehensive API reference documentation by discovering FastAPI endpoints and Pydantic schemas. Use when documenting REST APIs, creating endpoint summaries, generating schema definitions, or when user says "document API", "create API reference", or "list endpoints". Outputs to docs/api-reference.md.
- ddd-workflow:document-domain-uc: Create domain use case documents (DUC) for single-aggregate CRUD operations with flowchart diagrams, entity definitions, business rules, and acceptance criteria. file.content:
# Extract Agent

Extracts structured founder/investor information from profile text and
stores embeddings in pgvector for the matching engine to query.

Stack: FastAPI, OpenAI-compatible client (`openai`), `asyncpg` + pgvector.

## Run

```bash
uv sync
uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 3001   # http://localhost:3001
uv run pytest tests/e2e                            # requires Postgres (see below)
```

`test-curl.sh` has example requests against a running instance.

## Configuration

Copy `.env.example` to `.env`:

| Var | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `PORT` | Bind port (default `3001`) |
| `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_NAME`, `DB_USER`, `DB_PASSWORD`, `DB_SSL` | Postgres connection |
| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | API key for the chat/embedding provider — **never commit this**; keep it in `.env`/vault only |
| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible endpoint (defaults to an FPT Cloud marketplace gateway, not api.openai.com) |
| `OPENAI_CHAT_MODEL` | Extraction model (default `gpt-4o-mini`) |
| `OPENAI_EMBEDDING_MODEL`, `EMBEDDING_DIM` | Embedding model + vector dimension (default `text-embedding-3-small`, 1536) |

## Structure

- `app/main.py` — FastAPI app, lifespan (DB pool + schema init), health check, error handlers
- `app/config.py` — env-driven settings
- `app/db/` — asyncpg pool + `schema.sql` (run on startup)
- `app/routers/extract.py` — `/extract/*` and `/extracted/{userId}` routes
- `app/services/` — extraction (LLM), embedding (LLM or local feature-hash fallback), profile_source (maps gateway `profiles` rows to attributes), store (upsert/read `extracted_profiles`)
- `app/schemas/` — founder/investor JSON schemas for structured LLM output
- `tests/e2e/` — pytest end-to-end suite against a real Postgres (no DB/LLM mocking); see `tests/README.md`

Called by `backend/gateway` (`EXTRACT_SERVICE_URL`) to enrich a profile
after it's created or updated.

# Crawler Agent

Reserved for a source-crawling agent (pulling founder/investor public data
for `extract` to structure). Not yet implemented — empty scaffold directory.

See `backend/agent/extract/` for the sibling service this would feed, and
`ai-data-platform/` for the equivalent sourced/cited enrichment already

content.text:
`backend/agent/` holds the AI "agent" services that enrich profiles for the matching engine. It has two subdirectories:

## `extract/` — the Extract Agent (implemented)
A FastAPI service that turns free‑text founder/investor profiles into structured data + vector embeddings for matching.

- **What it does:** takes profile text → uses an LLM to extract structured founder/investor fields (per the JSON schemas in `app/schemas/`) → generates embeddings → upserts into Postgres/`pgvector` (`extracted_profiles`) so the matching engine can do similarity queries.
- **Stack:** FastAPI, OpenAI‑compatible client, `asyncpg` + pgvector.
- **Layout:**
  - `app/main.py` — app, DB pool lifespan, schema init, health check
  - `app/routers/extract.py` — `/extract/*` and `/extracted/{userId}` routes
  - `app/services/` — `extraction` (LLM), `embedding` (LLM **or** a local feature‑hash fallback when there's no key), `profile_source` (maps gateway `profiles` rows to attributes), `store` (read/upsert)
  - `app/schemas/` — founder/investor structured‑output schemas
  - `tests/e2e/` — pytest suite against a real Postgres
- **Who calls it:** `backend/gateway` calls it (via `EXTRACT_SERVICE_URL`) to enrich a profile after it's created/updated. Default port `3001`.
- **Note on the embedding fallback:** matches the CLAUDE.md gotcha — empty `OPENAI_API_KEY` → deterministic feature‑hash embeddings, so the profile→match flow still works without semantic embeddings.

## `crawler/` — the Crawler Agent (not implemented)
Just a scaffold with a README. Intended to crawl public founder/investor data to feed `extract`. Its README points to `ai-data-platform/` as the already‑built equivalent (sourced/cited enrichment) in the Python pipeline.

---

One thing worth flagging: your **working tree currently shows the old Node.js version of `extract` deleted** (all the `backend/agent/extract/src/*.js` files in `git status`), and the tree on disk is now the Python/FastAPI rewrite above — so this looks like a JS→Python migration that hasn't been committed yet. Want me to look at anything specific (the routes, the extraction logic, or the migration status)?

87	  1. Pull the requester's embedding, then fetch the `CANDIDATE_POOL` (default 50) nearest
88	     rows of the target role by pgvector cosine distance (`<=>`), with optional
89	     sector/stage/region pre-filters on the JSONB attributes.
90	  2. Compute `vectorScore = 1 − cosine_distance`.
91	  3. Compute `attributeScore` from explainable rules: **sector overlap** (Jaccard, ×0.4),
92	     **stage match** (+0.3), **geography match / global** (+0.2), **check size fits ask**
93	     (+0.05–0.1). Each firing rule adds a human-readable reason.
94	  4. Final `score = 0.7 · vectorScore + 0.3 · attributeScore` (weights configurable via

- DB access: **asyncpg** with raw SQL; an idempotent DDL bootstrap at startup keeps the
  schema aligned (and widens `users.role` with `admin`). Swagger docs are FastAPI-native
  at `/docs`.
- Owns the `users` and `profiles` tables.

### 2.2 Extract Agent — `backend/agent/extract` (:3001 canonical, **:3003 on this machine**)

Turns a profile (or raw text / a crawled page) into a canonical attribute set plus an
embedding, and writes one row per user into `extracted_profiles`.

- `POST /extract/profile` — load a gateway profile by `userId`, map it to canonical
  attributes, run the pipeline, upsert the row. (This is what the app calls.)
- `POST /extract/text`, `POST /extract/crawl` — same pipeline from raw text / a URL.
- `GET /extracted/:userId` — fetch the stored extraction.
- **Extraction** uses an OpenAI-compatible chat model with a strict JSON-schema response
  (`founder.schema.js` / `investor.schema.js`) against the FPT-cloud endpoint.
- **Embedding** uses `text-embedding-3-small` (1536-dim). If `OPENAI_API_KEY` is unset, it
  falls back to a deterministic **feature-hash embedding** (L2-normalized bag-of-words) so
  cosine ranking still works on lexical overlap — the pipeline never hard-fails.
- On boot it self-initializes its schema (`db/init.js` → `schema.sql`): creates the
  `vector` extension, the `extracted_profiles` table, and an **HNSW** cosine index.

> **Port note:** `.env.example` pins `PORT=3001`, but on the dev machine Uptime Kuma
> occupies 3001, so it runs on **3003** locally. The vite proxy and nginx tunnels are
> configured for 3003 accordingly.

### 2.3 Matching Engine — `backend/matching-engine` (:3002)

Ranks the opposite side of the market for a given user.

- `GET /matches/founders/:userId/investors` and
  `GET /matches/investors/:userId/founders` — the app's two live routes. Return `404` if
  the user has no `extracted_profiles` row yet (the app then triggers extraction and
  retries).
- `GET /matches` — **proxied** to the Python matching API (`MATCHING_API_URL`, default
  `:8000`) for the composite research-grade list.
- **Algorithm** (`matching.service.js` + `scoring.js`):
  1. Pull the requester's embedding, then fetch the `CANDIDATE_POOL` (default 50) nearest
     rows of the target role by pgvector cosine distance (`<=>`), with optional
     sector/stage/region pre-filters on the JSONB attributes.
  2. Compute `vectorScore = 1 − cosine_distance`.
  3. Compute `attributeScore` from explainable rules: **sector overlap** (Jaccard, ×0.4),
     **stage match** (+0.3), **geography match / global** (+0.2), **check size fits ask**
     (+0.05–0.1). Each firing rule adds a human-readable reason.
  4. Final `score = 0.7 · vectorScore + 0.3 · attributeScore` (weights configurable via
     `MATCH_VECTOR_WEIGHT` / `MATCH_ATTR_WEIGHT`), sorted descending, sliced to `limit`.

### 2.4 AI Data Platform — `ai-data-platform/` (Python)

A deterministic supervisor (event-sourced sagas, leased jobs, retries, dead-letter) that
drives isolated `feynman` / `pi` agent runtimes over stdio JSON-RPC for enrichment,
extraction, ranking, drafting, and verification — every fact cited, every draft
fact-checked. Exposes a FastAPI **read API + dashboard** on `:8000`
(`/entities /matches /sagas /jobs /events`). Full detail lives in its own docs:
[`ai-data-platform/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`](../ai-data-platform/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) and
[`AGENT_FLOWS.md`](../ai-data-platform/docs/AGENT_FLOWS.md).

## 3. Request flow — "show me my matches"

```
1. User completes profile      → Gateway  POST /profiles            (row in `profiles`)
2. App requests matches        → Matching GET  /matches/.../investors
3.   no extraction yet? (404)  → Extract  POST /extract/profile     (row in `extracted_profiles`)
4. App retries matches         → Matching GET  /matches/.../investors → ranked list + reasons
5. User opens a match          → outreach draft generated (client-side template)
```

The frontend encodes exactly this in `App.jsx` / `lib/api.js`: on a `404` from the matching
engine it auto-calls extraction, then retries the match request once.

content.text:
No — they're **separate, sibling services**, not one inside the other. Both live under `backend/` as peers:

```
backend/
├── agent/extract/     :3001 (3003 locally)  — Extract Agent
├── matching-engine/   :3002                 — Matching Engine
└── gateway/                                 — owns users/profiles
```

They're **coupled through a shared database table**, not code:

- **Extract** *writes* `extracted_profiles` — turns a profile into canonical attributes + an embedding, one row per user.
- **Matching Engine** *reads* `extracted_profiles` — pulls the requester's embedding, finds nearest candidates by pgvector cosine distance, then adds explainable attribute scoring.

The handoff is over HTTP at runtime (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:107`):

```
2. App → Matching  GET /matches/.../investors
3.   404 (no extraction yet)? → App → Extract  POST /extract/profile
4. App retries → Matching → ranked list
```

So the matching engine **depends on** extract's output, but extract is its own FastAPI app with its own port, Dockerfile, and pyproject — it can run and be called independently (the gateway calls it directly to enrich a profile after create/update). It also feeds the Python `ai-data-platform/` (`:8000`), which the matching engine proxies for its research-grade `GET /matches` list.

Think of `extract` as the **producer** of the matching substrate, and `matching-engine` as one of its **consumers** — related, but not the same service.

file.content:
# VietNexus — Architecture

How the system is put together: services, request flow, data model, and deployment.
For the *why* behind each technology choice, see [`TECHNOLOGY.md`](TECHNOLOGY.md).

---

## 1. System at a glance

```
                         ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
   Web (Vite/React) ─┐   │                 VPS nginx                  │
                     ├──▶│  TLS termination · path routing · tunnels  │
   Mobile (Expo)  ───┘   │           https://dqplus.ddns.net          │
                         └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                            │  /api/*
             ┌──────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┐
             ▼                               ▼                               ▼
     ┌───────────────┐             ┌───────────────────┐          ┌───────────────────┐
     │   Gateway     │             │  Extract Agent    │          │  Matching Engine  │
     │  :3000        │             │  :3001 (:3003 loc)│          │  :3002            │
     │  auth+profiles│             │  attrs+embeddings │          │  vector+attr rank │
     └──────┬────────┘             └─────────┬─────────┘          └─────────┬─────────┘
            │  profiles                       │ extracted_profiles          │ reads
            ▼                                 ▼                             ▼
     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
     │           Postgres 16 + pgvector  (DB: dealflow · shared by all services)        │
     └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                            ▲
                                            │  same DB
                    ┌───────────────────────┴────────────────────────┐
                    │   AI Data Platform (Python spine + agents)      │
                    │   FastAPI read API :8000 · supervisor · sagas   │
                    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Three Python/FastAPI services own the app's live path; a Python spine (the AI Data Platform)
shares the same Postgres for research-grade, evidence-cited matchmaking. `nginx` on the VPS
is the single public entry point and routes by URL prefix.

## 2. Services

### 2.1 Gateway — `backend/gateway` (:3000)

The front door for identity and profile data.

- **Auth**: `POST /auth/register`, `POST /auth/login` (bcrypt + JWT), `GET /auth/me`.
- **Profiles** (JWT-protected): `POST /profiles`, `GET /profiles/:id`, `PATCH /profiles/:id`.
- **Health**: `GET /health` (checks DB connectivity).

lastPrompt: those work are belong to ai-data-platform, right?

content.text:
No — it's actually the **other way around**, and they're **two parallel implementations of the same idea**, not one owning the other.

There are two distinct stacks in this repo:

**1. The `backend/` trio — the app's live path**
- Gateway (`:3000`), Extract Agent (`:3001`), Matching Engine (`:3002`)
- Lightweight, request-driven. Extract writes `extracted_profiles`, Matching reads it. This is what the frontend actually hits for "show me my matches."

**2. `ai-data-platform/` — the Python spine (`:8000`)**
- A heavier, deterministic supervisor (event-sourced sagas, leased jobs, retries, dead-letter) driving agent runtimes for enrichment, extraction, ranking, drafting, verification — **every fact cited, every draft fact-checked**. This is the "research-grade" version.

**What connects them:** they **share the same Postgres** (`dealflow`), not code (`ARCHITECTURE.md:29-34`). They're peers on the same database. The only crossover is that the Matching Engine's `GET /matches` route **proxies out** to the AI Data Platform (`MATCHING_API_URL`, `:8000`) when it wants the composite research-grade list (`ARCHITECTURE.md:84`).

So:
- `extract` and `matching-engine` are **their own services** — the fast, live path.
- `ai-data-platform` is a **separate, more rigorous engine** that does overlapping work (extraction + ranking) but with citations/verification, sharing the same DB.

The `crawler/` README even hints at this framing — it calls `ai-data-platform/` the "equivalent sourced/cited enrichment already built in the Python matching pipeline."

Do you want me to trace exactly where the `backend` path hands off to `ai-data-platform` (the proxy in the matching engine), so you can see how much is duplicated vs. shared?

message.content: should be belong to data platform :))

content.text:
Ha, yeah — architecturally that's a fair take. Right now the repo has **two engines doing overlapping work** (extract + rank), which is real duplication:

- `backend/agent/extract` + `backend/matching-engine` — the fast, thin, live path (JS-era logic, now being rewritten to Python per your uncommitted diff).
- `ai-data-platform` — the rigorous version of the *same* pipeline (extraction + ranking) but event-sourced, cited, and fact-checked.

If you folded the `backend` extract/match logic **into** the data platform, you'd get one source of truth for "profile → attributes → embedding → ranked matches," and the `backend` services would shrink to thin HTTP adapters (or disappear). The matching engine already half-admits this — its `GET /matches` just **proxies to `:8000`** for the "real" list.

The one reason they were kept split is **latency**: the live app needs a sub-second 404→extract→retry loop, and the data platform's saga/leased-job machinery is built for correctness, not interactive speed. So the honest end-state is probably:

- **Data platform owns the logic** (the durable, cited pipeline).
- **Backend keeps a thin read path** — either a fast cache/read-model the platform populates, or a synchronous "quick mode" endpoint on the platform itself.

Is this just an observation, or do you want to actually move toward consolidating them? If the latter, I'd start by mapping exactly what `extract` + `matching-engine` do that `ai-data-platform` doesn't already cover — that gap is the real migration scope.

file.content:
"""Port of src/routes/match.routes.js — keep endpoint behavior identical."""

import httpx
from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException, Request
from fastapi.responses import Response

from app import config
from app.services.matching import find_matches

router = APIRouter()


def parse_limit(value: str | None) -> int:
    try:
        n = float(value) if value not in (None, "") else 0
    except ValueError:
        n = 0
    if not n:
        n = 10
    return min(max(n, 1), 50)


# Forward bare GET /matches (and its query string) to the Python API. nginx maps /api/matches here
# after stripping /api, so this is what dqplus.ddns.net/api/matches resolves to. The two
# /matches/{role} routes below are unaffected.
@router.get("/matches")
async def proxy_matches(request: Request):
    search = f"?{request.url.query}" if request.url.query else ""
    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=8.0) as client:
            upstream = await client.get(
                f"{config.MATCHING_API_URL}/matches{search}",
                headers={"Accept": "application/json"},
            )
    except httpx.HTTPError as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail=str(exc) or "Internal server error")

    return Response(
        content=upstream.content,
        status_code=upstream.status_code,
        media_type=upstream.headers.get("content-type", "application/json"),
    )


@router.get("/matches/founders/{user_id}/investors")
async def founders_to_investors(
    user_id: str,
    limit: str | None = None,
    sector: str | None = None,
    stage: str | None = None,
    region: str | None = None,
):
    result = await find_matches(
        user_id=user_id,
        target_role="investor",
        limit=parse_limit(limit),
        filters={"sector": sector, "stage": stage, "region": region},
    )
    return result


@router.get("/matches/investors/{user_id}/founders")
async def investors_to_founders(
    user_id: str,
    limit: str | None = None,
    sector: str | None = None,
    stage: str | None = None,
    region: str | None = None,
):
    result = await find_matches(
        user_id=user_id,
        target_role="founder",
        limit=parse_limit(limit),
        filters={"sector": sector, "stage": stage, "region": region},
    )
    return result

1	"""Port of src/services/matching.service.js — keep query/scoring behavior identical."""
2	
3	import json
4	
5	from fastapi import HTTPException
6	
7	from app import config
8	from app.db import get_pool
9	from app.services.scoring import score_match
10	
11	
12	async def find_matches(user_id: str, target_role: str, limit: int, filters: dict | None = None):
13	    filters = filters or {}
14	    pool = get_pool()
15	
16	    me_rows = await pool.fetch(
17	        "SELECT role, attributes FROM extracted_profiles WHERE user_id = $1", user_id
18	    )
19	    if not me_rows:
20	        raise HTTPException(
21	            status_code=404, detail="No extracted profile for user; run extraction first"
22	        )
23	    me = me_rows[0]
24	    me_role = me["role"]
25	    me_attrs = json.loads(me["attributes"])
26	
27	    conditions = ["ep.role = $2", "ep.user_id <> $1"]
28	    params = [user_id, target_role]
29	
30	    sector_key = "sectors" if target_role == "investor" else "industry"
31	    stage_key = "stages" if target_role == "investor" else "stage"
32	    region_key = "geographies" if target_role == "investor" else "target_regions"
33	
34	    if filters.get("sector"):
35	        params.append(filters["sector"].lower())
36	        conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->'{sector_key}' ? ${len(params)}")
37	    if filters.get("stage"):
38	        params.append(filters["stage"].lower())
39	        if target_role == "investor":
40	            conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->'{stage_key}' ? ${len(params)}")
41	        else:
42	            conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->>'{stage_key}' = ${len(params)}")
43	    if filters.get("region"):
44	        params.append(filters["region"].lower())
45	        conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->'{region_key}' ? ${len(params)}")
46	
47	    query = f"""
48	        WITH me AS (
49	          SELECT embedding FROM extracted_profiles WHERE user_id = $1
50	        )
51	        SELECT ep.user_id, ep.attributes,
52	               1 - (ep.embedding <=> me.embedding) AS vector_score
53	        FROM extracted_profiles ep, me
54	        WHERE {' AND '.join(conditions)}
55	        ORDER BY ep.embedding <=> me.embedding
56	        LIMIT {config.MATCH_CANDIDATE_POOL}
57	    """
58	    candidates = await pool.fetch(query, *params)
59	
60	    matches = []
61	    for c in candidates:
62	        vector_score = float(c["vector_score"])
63	        candidate_attrs = json.loads(c["attributes"])
64	        scored = score_match(me_role, me_attrs, candidate_attrs)
65	        attribute_score = scored["attributeScore"]
66	        matches.append(
67	            {
68	                "userId": c["user_id"],
69	                "score": round(
70	                    config.MATCH_VECTOR_WEIGHT * vector_score
71	                    + config.MATCH_ATTR_WEIGHT * attribute_score,
72	                    4,
73	                ),
74	                "vectorScore": round(vector_score, 4),
75	                "attributeScore": round(attribute_score, 4),
76	                "attributes": candidate_attrs,
77	                "reasons": scored["reasons"],
78	            }
79	        )
80	
81	    matches.sort(key=lambda m: m["score"], reverse=True)
82	    matches = matches[:limit]
83	
84	    return {"userId": user_id, "role": me_role, "matches": matches}

"""Port of src/services/matching.service.js — keep query/scoring behavior identical."""

import json

from fastapi import HTTPException

from app import config
from app.db import get_pool
from app.services.scoring import score_match


async def find_matches(user_id: str, target_role: str, limit: int, filters: dict | None = None):
    filters = filters or {}
    pool = get_pool()

    me_rows = await pool.fetch(
        "SELECT role, attributes FROM extracted_profiles WHERE user_id = $1", user_id
    )
    if not me_rows:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=404, detail="No extracted profile for user; run extraction first"
        )
    me = me_rows[0]
    me_role = me["role"]
    me_attrs = json.loads(me["attributes"])

    conditions = ["ep.role = $2", "ep.user_id <> $1"]
    params = [user_id, target_role]

    sector_key = "sectors" if target_role == "investor" else "industry"
    stage_key = "stages" if target_role == "investor" else "stage"
    region_key = "geographies" if target_role == "investor" else "target_regions"

    if filters.get("sector"):
        params.append(filters["sector"].lower())
        conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->'{sector_key}' ? ${len(params)}")
    if filters.get("stage"):
        params.append(filters["stage"].lower())
        if target_role == "investor":
            conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->'{stage_key}' ? ${len(params)}")
        else:
            conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->>'{stage_key}' = ${len(params)}")
    if filters.get("region"):
        params.append(filters["region"].lower())
        conditions.append(f"ep.attributes->'{region_key}' ? ${len(params)}")

    query = f"""
        WITH me AS (
          SELECT embedding FROM extracted_profiles WHERE user_id = $1
        )
        SELECT ep.user_id, ep.attributes,
               1 - (ep.embedding <=> me.embedding) AS vector_score
        FROM extracted_profiles ep, me
        WHERE {' AND '.join(conditions)}
        ORDER BY ep.embedding <=> me.embedding
        LIMIT {config.MATCH_CANDIDATE_POOL}
    """
    candidates = await pool.fetch(query, *params)

    matches = []
    for c in candidates:
        vector_score = float(c["vector_score"])
        candidate_attrs = json.loads(c["attributes"])
        scored = score_match(me_role, me_attrs, candidate_attrs)
        attribute_score = scored["attributeScore"]
        matches.append(
            {
                "userId": c["user_id"],
                "score": round(
                    config.MATCH_VECTOR_WEIGHT * vector_score
                    + config.MATCH_ATTR_WEIGHT * attribute_score,
                    4,
                ),
                "vectorScore": round(vector_score, 4),
                "attributeScore": round(attribute_score, 4),
                "attributes": candidate_attrs,
                "reasons": scored["reasons"],
            }
        )

    matches.sort(key=lambda m: m["score"], reverse=True)
    matches = matches[:limit]

    return {"userId": user_id, "role": me_role, "matches": matches}

1	"""FastAPI read API + dashboard (spec §8, §13, Phase 3).
2	
3	Read-only projections over Postgres for the dashboard, plus POST /runs to trigger
4	an outreach run. Endpoints (spec §8): /entities /matches /sagas /jobs /events
5	/workers /metrics /costs + POST /runs. The supervisor (python -m spine.supervisor
6	--drain) drains any jobs this enqueues; the API itself never touches agents.
7	
8	Run: uv run uvicorn api.app:app --port 8000
9	"""
10	from __future__ import annotations
11	
12	import base64
13	import hashlib
14	import hmac
15	import json
16	import os
17	import time
18	import uuid
19	from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
20	from decimal import Decimal
21	from pathlib import Path
22	from typing import Any
23	
24	from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Query, Request
25	from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
26	from fastapi.responses import FileResponse, JSONResponse, RedirectResponse
27	
28	from spine import outreach, telemetry
29	from spine.store import Store
30	
31	log = telemetry.get_logger("api")
32	_DASHBOARD = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "dashboard" / "index.html"
33	
34	# Partner entity types (spec §5.1). Everything else is a startup.
35	PARTNER_TYPES = ["investor", "corporation", "university", "research_institution"]
36	
37	
38	@asynccontextmanager
39	async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
40	    telemetry.configure()
41	    app.state.store = await Store.connect(min_size=1, max_size=5)
42	    log.info("api_started")
43	    try:
44	        yield
45	    finally:
46	        await app.state.store.close()
47	
48	
49	app = FastAPI(title="Deal-flow Matchmaker", lifespan=lifespan)
50	
51	# Permissive CORS: read-only API, no cookie auth — wildcard origin is safe only with
52	# allow_credentials=False (browsers reject "*" + credentials).
53	app.add_middleware(
54	    CORSMiddleware,
55	    allow_origins=["*"],
56	    allow_methods=["*"],
57	    allow_headers=["*"],
58	    allow_credentials=False,
59	)
60	
61	
62	def _pool(app: FastAPI):
63	    return app.state.store.pool
64	
65	
66	def _jsonable(v: Any) -> Any:
67	    """Normalize asyncpg row values: Decimal -> float, jsonb text -> parsed."""
68	    if isinstance(v, Decimal):
69	        return float(v)
70	    return v
71	
72	
73	def _row(record) -> dict:
74	    return {k: _jsonable(v) for k, v in dict(record).items()}
75	
76	
77	def _parse_jsonb(d: dict, *fields: str) -> dict:
78	    for f in fields:
79	        if isinstance(d.get(f), str):
80	            try:
81	                d[f] = json.loads(d[f])
82	            except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
83	                pass
84	    return d
85	
86	
87	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
88	# dashboard — admin-only when JWT_SECRET is set (gateway-issued HS256 tokens).
89	# Admins exist only via direct DB update (UPDATE users SET role='admin'); the
90	# gateway never registers them. Data endpoints below stay public — the frontend
91	# match flow reads /matches directly.
92	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
93	
94	_JWT_SECRET = [REDACTED]("JWT_SECRET", "")
95	_ADMIN_COOKIE = "vn_admin"
96	
97	
98	def _b64url_decode(seg: str) -> bytes:
99	    return base64.urlsafe_b64decode(seg + "=" * (-len(seg) % 4))
100	
101	
102	def _verify_admin_jwt(token: str) -> bool:
103	    """Verify a gateway HS256 JWT and require role=admin. stdlib-only on purpose:
104	    this service otherwise has no JWT dependency."""
105	    try:
106	        header_b64, payload_b64, sig_b64 = token.split(".")
107	        if json.loads(_b64url_decode(header_b64)).get("alg") != "HS256":
108	            return False
109	        expected = hmac.new(
110	            _JWT_SECRET.encode(), f"{header_b64}.{payload_b64}".encode(), hashlib.sha256
111	        ).digest()
112	        if not hmac.compare_digest(expected, _b64url_decode(sig_b64)):
113	            return False
114	        payload = json.loads(_b64url_decode(payload_b64))
115	        if payload.get("exp") is not None and time.time() >= float(payload["exp"]):
116	            return False
117	        return payload.get("role") == "admin"
118	    except Exception:
119	        return False
120	
121	
122	@app.get("/", include_in_schema=False)
123	async def dashboard(request: Request):
124	    if not _DASHBOARD.exists():
125	        raise HTTPException(404, "dashboard not built")
126	    if _JWT_SECRET:
127	        token = [REDACTED]("token")
128	        if token:
129	            if not _verify_admin_jwt(token):
130	                raise HTTPException(403, "admin role required")
131	            resp = RedirectResponse("/", status_code=303)
132	            resp.set_cookie(_ADMIN_COOKIE, token, httponly=True, samesite="lax")
133	            return resp
134	        auth = request.headers.get("authorization", "")
135	        token = [REDACTED](" ", 1)[1] if auth.lower().startswith("bearer ") else None
136	        token = token or request.cookies.get(_ADMIN_COOKIE)
137	        if not token:
138	            raise HTTPException(401, "admin token required: open /?token=<admin jwt>")
139	        if not _verify_admin_jwt(token):
140	            raise HTTPException(403, "admin role required")
141	    return FileResponse(_DASHBOARD)
142	
143	
144	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
145	# entities
146	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
147	
148	@app.get("/entities")
149	async def entities(type: str | None = Query(None), status: str | None = Query(None)):
150	    clauses, args = [], []
151	    if type:
152	        args.append(type)
153	        clauses.append(f"type = ${len(args)}")
154	    if status:
155	        args.append(status)
156	        clauses.append(f"status = ${len(args)}")
157	    where = ("WHERE " + " AND ".join(clauses)) if clauses else ""
158	    rows = await _pool(app).fetch(
159	        "SELECT e.id, e.type, e.name, e.status, "
160	        "(SELECT count(*) FROM matches m WHERE m.startup_id = e.id OR m.partner_id = e.id) "
161	        "AS match_count "
162	        f"FROM entities e {where} ORDER BY e.type, e.name",
163	        *args,
164	    )
165	    return [_row(r) for r in rows]
166	
167	
168	@app.get("/entities/{entity_id}")
169	async def entity(entity_id: str):
170	    pool = _pool(app)
171	    row = await pool.fetchrow(
172	        "SELECT id, type, name, status, profile, created_at, updated_at "
173	        "FROM entities WHERE id = $1",
174	        entity_id,
175	    )
176	    if row is None:
177	        raise HTTPException(404, "entity not found")
178	    # Relationship graph (§5.1 edges): outgoing from this entity + incoming resolved ones,
179	    # with the counterpart's display name when it has been onboarded.
180	    edge_rows = await pool.fetch(
181	        "SELECT e.src_id, e.dst_id, e.dst_name, e.dst_resolved, e.kind, e.source_url, "
182	        "s.name AS src_name, d.name AS dst_entity_name "
183	        "FROM edges e LEFT JOIN entities s ON s.id = e.src_id "
184	        "LEFT JOIN entities d ON d.id = e.dst_id AND e.dst_resolved "
185	        "WHERE e.src_id = $1 OR (e.dst_id = $1 AND e.dst_resolved) "
186	        "ORDER BY e.kind",
187	        entity_id,
188	    )
189	    out = _parse_jsonb(_row(row), "profile")
190	    out["edges"] = [
191	        {**_row(r), "direction": "out" if r["src_id"] == entity_id else "in"}
192	        for r in edge_rows
193	    ]
194	    return out
195	
196	
197	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
198	# matches
199	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
200	
201	@app.get("/matches")
202	async def matches(startup_id: str | None = Query(None),
203	                  partner_id: str | None = Query(None),
204	                  status: str | None = Query(None)):
205	    """Ranked matches, composite-desc. Filter by startup_id for a startup's ranked partners,
206	    or by partner_id for the reverse view (startups that matched to a partner) — A4."""
207	    clauses, args = [], []
208	    if startup_id:
209	        args.append(startup_id)
210	        clauses.append(f"m.startup_id = ${len(args)}")
211	    if partner_id:
212	        args.append(partner_id)
213	        clauses.append(f"m.partner_id = ${len(args)}")
214	    if status:
215	        args.append(status)
216	        clauses.append(f"m.status = ${len(args)}")
217	    where = ("WHERE " + " AND ".join(clauses)) if clauses else ""
218	    rows = await _pool(app).fetch(
219	        "SELECT m.id, m.startup_id, s.name AS startup_name, "
220	        "m.partner_id, p.name AS partner_name, p.type AS partner_type, "
221	        "m.composite, m.semantic, m.sector_overlap, m.rationale_en, m.rationale_vi, "
222	        "m.draft_en, m.draft_vi, m.status, m.created_at "
223	        "FROM matches m LEFT JOIN entities p ON p.id = m.partner_id "
224	        "LEFT JOIN entities s ON s.id = m.startup_id "
225	        f"{where} ORDER BY m.startup_id, m.composite DESC NULLS LAST",
226	        *args,
227	    )
228	    return [_row(r) for r in rows]
229	
230	
231	@app.get("/matches_v2")
232	async def matches_v2(startup_id: str | None = Query(None),
233	                     partner_id: str | None = Query(None),
234	                     status: str | None = Query(None)):
235	    """Dry-run matches from the FPT-hosted re-rank (matches_v2). Same shape as /matches
236	    plus a `model` column; no draft columns. Filter by startup_id or partner_id."""
237	    clauses, args = [], []
238	    if startup_id:
239	        args.append(startup_id)
240	        clauses.append(f"m.startup_id = ${len(args)}")
241	    if partner_id:
242	        args.append(partner_id)
243	        clauses.append(f"m.partner_id = ${len(args)}")
244	    if status:
245	        args.append(status)
246	        clauses.append(f"m.status = ${len(args)}")
247	    where = ("WHERE " + " AND ".join(clauses)) if clauses else ""
248	    rows = await _pool(app).fetch(
249	        "SELECT m.id, m.startup_id, s.name AS startup_name, "
250	        "m.partner_id, p.name AS partner_name, p.type AS partner_type, "
251	        "m.composite, m.semantic, m.sector_overlap, m.rationale_en, m.rationale_vi, "
252	        "m.model, m.status, m.created_at "
253	        "FROM matches_v2 m LEFT JOIN entities p ON p.id = m.partner_id "
254	        "LEFT JOIN entities s ON s.id = m.startup_id "
255	        f"{where} ORDER BY m.startup_id, m.composite DESC NULLS LAST",
256	        *args,
257	    )
258	    return [_row(r) for r in rows]
259	
260	
261	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
262	# sagas / jobs / events
263	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
264	
265	@app.get("/sagas")
266	async def sagas(type: str | None = Query(None)):
267	    clauses, args = [], []
268	    if type:
269	        args.append(type)
270	        clauses.append(f"type = ${len(args)}")
271	    where = ("WHERE " + " AND ".join(clauses)) if clauses else ""
272	    rows = await _pool(app).fetch(
273	        "SELECT saga_id, type, subject_id, current_step, status, trace_id, updated_at "
274	        f"FROM saga_instances {where} ORDER BY updated_at DESC",
275	        *args,
276	    )
277	    return [_row(r) for r in rows]
278	
279	
280	@app.get("/jobs")
281	async def jobs(status: str | None = Query(None), saga_id: str | None = Query(None)):
282	    clauses, args = [], []
283	    if status:
284	        args.append(status)
285	        clauses.append(f"status = ${len(args)}")
286	    if saga_id:
287	        args.append(saga_id)
288	        clauses.append(f"saga_id = ${len(args)}")
289	    where = ("WHERE " + " AND ".join(clauses)) if clauses else ""
290	    rows = await _pool(app).fetch(
291	        "SELECT id, saga_id, stage, target_id, agent, status, attempts, leased_by, "
292	        "lease_expires_at, queued_at, leased_at, done_at "
293	        f"FROM jobs {where} ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 200",
294	        *args,
295	    )
296	    return [_row(r) for r in rows]
297	
298	
299	@app.get("/events")
300	async def events(saga_id: str | None = Query(None), limit: int = Query(50, le=500)):
301	    clauses, args = [], []
302	    if saga_id:
303	        args.append(saga_id)
304	        clauses.append(f"saga_id = ${len(args)}")
305	    where = ("WHERE " + " AND ".join(clauses)) if clauses else ""
306	    args.append(limit)
307	    rows = await _pool(app).fetch(
308	        "SELECT id, saga_id, seq, kind, trace_id, payload, created_at "
309	        f"FROM events {where} ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT ${len(args)}",
310	        *args,
311	    )
312	    return [_parse_jsonb(_row(r), "payload") for r in rows]
313	
314	
315	@app.get("/workers")
316	async def workers():
317	    rows = await _pool(app).fetch(
318	        "SELECT worker_id, agent_type, runtime, pid, status, current_job_id, "
319	        "jobs_since_reset, restart_count, started_at, last_heartbeat_at "
320	        "FROM workers ORDER BY started_at DESC LIMIT 100"
321	    )
322	    return [_row(r) for r in rows]
323	
324	
325	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
326	# observability: /metrics (pool + queue health) and /costs (llm_usage aggregates)
327	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
328	
329	@app.get("/metrics")
330	async def metrics():
331	    """§13.3 — pool health (workers by status) + queue health (jobs by stage/status)."""
332	    pool = _pool(app)
333	    worker_rows = await pool.fetch(
334	        "SELECT status, count(*) AS n FROM workers GROUP BY status"
335	    )
336	    job_rows = await pool.fetch(
337	        "SELECT stage, status, count(*) AS n FROM jobs GROUP BY stage, status "
338	        "ORDER BY stage, status"
339	    )
340	    saga_rows = await pool.fetch(
341	        "SELECT type, status, count(*) AS n FROM saga_instances GROUP BY type, status"
342	    )
343	    dead = await pool.fetchval("SELECT count(*) FROM jobs WHERE status = 'dead'")
344	    ready = await pool.fetchval("SELECT count(*) FROM jobs WHERE status = 'ready'")
345	    leased = await pool.fetchval("SELECT count(*) FROM jobs WHERE status = 'leased'")
346	    return {
347	        "workers": {r["status"]: r["n"] for r in worker_rows},
348	        "jobs_by_stage_status": [_row(r) for r in job_rows],
349	        "queue": {"ready": ready, "leased": leased, "dead_letter": dead},
350	        "sagas": [_row(r) for r in saga_rows],
351	    }
352	
353	
354	@app.get("/costs")
355	async def costs():
356	    """§13.2 — running LLM cost total + per-agent/saga/model breakdown, and the two
357	    headline demo numbers: cost-per-onboarded-entity and cost-per-match."""
358	    pool = _pool(app)
359	    totals = await pool.fetchrow(
360	        "SELECT COALESCE(SUM(cost_usd),0) AS cost_usd, COALESCE(SUM(tokens_in),0) AS tokens_in, "
361	        "COALESCE(SUM(tokens_out),0) AS tokens_out, count(*) AS turns FROM llm_usage"
362	    )
363	    by_agent = await pool.fetch(
364	        "SELECT agent, runtime, COALESCE(SUM(cost_usd),0) AS cost_usd, "
365	        "COALESCE(SUM(tokens_in),0) AS tokens_in, COALESCE(SUM(tokens_out),0) AS tokens_out, "
366	        "count(*) AS turns FROM llm_usage GROUP BY agent, runtime ORDER BY cost_usd DESC"
367	    )
368	    by_model = await pool.fetch(
369	        "SELECT model, COALESCE(SUM(cost_usd),0) AS cost_usd, count(*) AS turns "
370	        "FROM llm_usage GROUP BY model ORDER BY cost_usd DESC"
371	    )
372	    by_saga = await pool.fetch(
373	        "SELECT saga_id, COALESCE(SUM(cost_usd),0) AS cost_usd, count(*) AS turns "
374	        "FROM llm_usage GROUP BY saga_id ORDER BY cost_usd DESC LIMIT 50"
375	    )
376	    ready_entities = await pool.fetchval(
377	        "SELECT count(*) FROM entities WHERE status = 'ready'"
378	    )
379	    match_count = await pool.fetchval("SELECT count(*) FROM matches")
380	    total_cost = float(totals["cost_usd"])
381	    return {
382	        "total": {
383	            "cost_usd": total_cost,
384	            "tokens_in": totals["tokens_in"],
385	            "tokens_out": totals["tokens_out"],
386	            "turns": totals["turns"],
387	        },
388	        "cost_per_ready_entity": round(total_cost / ready_entities, 6) if ready_entities else None,
389	        "cost_per_match": round(total_cost / match_count, 6) if match_count else None,
390	        "by_agent": [_row(r) for r in by_agent],
391	        "by_model": [_row(r) for r in by_model],
392	        "by_saga": [_row(r) for r in by_saga],
393	    }
394	
395	
396	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
397	# POST /runs — trigger an outreach run (R4)
398	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
399	
400	@app.post("/runs")
401	async def create_run(body: dict | None = None):
402	    """Trigger outreach for one startup (`startup_id`) or every ready startup (`all`).
403	    Enqueues jobs; the running supervisor (--drain) advances them. Returns the trace_id
404	    and the sagas started. Idempotent on saga/job keys — re-triggering is safe."""
405	    body = body or {}
406	    store: Store = app.state.store
407	    pool = store.pool
408	    trace_id = "run:" + uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]
409	
410	    if body.get("all"):
411	        rows = await pool.fetch(
412	            "SELECT id FROM entities WHERE type = 'startup' AND status = 'ready' ORDER BY id"
413	        )
414	        ids = [r["id"] for r in rows]
415	    elif body.get("startup_id"):
416	        ids = [body["startup_id"]]
417	    else:
418	        raise HTTPException(400, "pass {\"startup_id\": <id>} or {\"all\": true}")
419	
420	    if not ids:
421	        raise HTTPException(409, "no ready startups to run")
422	
423	    sagas_started = []
424	    for sid in ids:
425	        saga_id = await outreach.start(store, sid, trace_id=trace_id)
426	        sagas_started.append(saga_id)
427	    log.info("run_triggered", trace_id=trace_id, count=len(sagas_started))
428	    return JSONResponse(
429	        status_code=202,
430	        content={"trace_id": trace_id, "sagas": sagas_started, "count": len(sagas_started)},
431	    )
432
